title: SOP Management: Keeping Procedures Current and Used
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: SOP management for UK and EU pharma: keep procedures current, controlled and actually used at the point of use, in line with EU GMP, ICH Q10 and ALCOA+.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette set against deep navy, with a single subtle green accent light. A pristine, softly lit quality documentation area: a gloved hand resting on a sleek tablet that displays a controlled procedure, beside an orderly row of neatly tabbed document binders on a brushed-steel shelf, their edges catching cool light. Soft natural light falls from a high window across an immaculate work surface, with a blurred laboratory or production corridor receding behind. Shallow depth of field draws focus to the tablet and the nearest binder spine while the background dissolves into calm, cool shadow. Quietly premium, authoritative and orderly. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: How often should pharmaceutical SOPs be reviewed?
faqs.a: There is no single regulated interval, but a periodic review cycle of every two to three years is common practice, set on a risk basis so that higher-impact procedures are reviewed more frequently. Critical, complex or frequently changing processes may warrant annual review, while stable, low-risk procedures can sit at the longer end. The key expectation under EU GMP Chapter 4 is that the cycle is defined, documented and genuinely actioned, not that a fixed number is met.

faqs.q: What is the difference between periodic review and change control for SOPs?
faqs.a: Periodic review is a scheduled, time-based check that asks whether an existing procedure still reflects current practice, equipment and regulations, regardless of whether anything has changed. Change control is event-driven and triggered by a specific need to revise, such as a deviation, CAPA, new equipment or a regulatory update like the revised Annex 1. A mature SOP management system runs both: the calendar catches quiet drift, while change control manages active revisions and their impact on training and linked documents.

faqs.q: How do you make sure staff actually follow SOPs?
faqs.a: Start by writing concise, task-focused procedures that describe how the work is really done, then assign training tied to each effective version, using competence assessment rather than read-and-understand alone for critical tasks. Make the current controlled version frictionless to access at the point of use so operators are never tempted to rely on old printouts or memory. Finally, verify alignment through self-inspection and routine supervision, and feed observations back into the document so the procedure stays trusted and used.
